Size bonding conductors from the code tables based on the overcurrent device. Metal raceway can serve as the bond if its joints are made up tight. Water meters get jumpers; isolated sections get connected.
Bonding the water service: clamp upstream of the meter, jumper across it, conductor sized to the table — because a corroded meter gasket shouldn't decide whether a fault clears.

Line L.Quiz: Installing bonding: conductor sizing, raceway continuity, water and gas piping bonds.

Line Q.Title: Describe Raceway and Cable Types

Summary.Content: Conductors travel in raceways (EMT, rigid, PVC, flexible conduits) or as cables (NMD90, armoured AC90/TECK, underground types) — each with an environment it's designed for and rules that follow.

EMT for dry interiors; rigid where damage threatens; PVC underground and in corrosive areas; NMD90 in dry residential walls; TECK where things get industrial. Fill tables limit how many conductors share a raceway.
Choosing between EMT and TECK for a parkade run comes down to environment: moisture, mechanical damage and what the spec already decided. The catalogue is big; the drawings narrow it.

Line Q.Quiz: Raceway and cable types: EMT, rigid, PVC, NMD90, AC90, TECK and where each belongs.

Line Q.Title: Bend and Install Conduit

Summary.Content: Conduit bending is the trade's signature craft: 90s, offsets, saddles and kicks measured and bent so runs land square, parallel and within the 360° bend limit between pulls.

Learn your bender's take-up and deduct. Offsets use paired bends and multiplier tables. Total bends between pull points stay at or under 360°. Support spacing and strap requirements come from code tables.
A clean rack of parallel offsets around a beam is the difference between a journeyperson's run and a fight with the wire during every future pull.

Line Q.Quiz: Conduit bending: 90s, offsets, saddles, the 360-degree rule and support spacing.

Line Q.Title: Pull and Terminate Cables

Summary.Content: Getting conductors through raceway without damage takes planning: swab the run, use pulling compound, respect maximum pulling tension and leave enough tail for terminations.

Pull together whatever shares the raceway — never add to an occupied conduit. Lubricate long or bendy pulls. Support cable trays and secure cables at code intervals. Label both ends before walking away.
A four-hundred-foot feeder pull: fish line, then rope, then compound-slicked conductors with two people feeding and one on the tugger — and tails labelled before lunch.

Line Q.Quiz: Cable pulling: fill and tension limits, lubrication, supports and labelling.

Line Q.Title: Install Boxes and Enclosures

Summary.Content: Every splice, device and termination lives in a box or enclosure sized for its contents, anchored to structure and matched to its environment.

Box fill is calculated by conductor count and size — crowd a box and code is failed and splices suffer. Weatherproof boxes and covers guard wet locations. Boxes stay accessible; burying one behind drywall is a code violation with a long memory.
Roughing in a kitchen: deep boxes where three cables meet, vapour hats on outside walls, and every box flush to the coming drywall line — measured, not guessed.

Line Q.Quiz: Boxes and enclosures: fill calculations, environmental ratings, accessibility and mounting.

Line R.Title: Describe Branch Circuit Requirements

Summary.Content: Branch circuits are the final run from the last overcurrent device to the outlets. The CEC prescribes their conductor sizes, outlet counts and the dedicated circuits certain loads demand.

15 A circuits use 14 AWG copper and carry up to 12 outlets. Kitchens, bathrooms, laundry and fridges get dedicated treatment. Receptacle spacing rules ensure no point along a wall is far from an outlet.
Circuiting a bedroom floor plan: count outlets toward the 12 limit, keep lighting and receptacles sensibly split, and give the future window-AC receptacle its own run now, not later.

Line R.Quiz: Branch circuits: conductor sizing, outlet limits, dedicated circuits and receptacle spacing.

Line R.Title: Describe Switching Arrangements

Summary.Content: Switching schemes range from a single-pole controlling one light to 3-way and 4-way systems controlling loads from many points, plus dimmers and the smart controls layered on top.
Single-pole: two terminals, one location. 3-way: three terminals, two locations, travellers between. 4-way: sits between 3-ways to add locations without limit. Dimmers must match their load type.
A hallway lit from both ends is the classic 3-way; add a mid-hall entry and a 4-way drops between them. The traveller pair is the thread through all of it.

Line R.Quiz: Switching: single-pole, 3-way and 4-way circuits, travellers and dimmer compatibility.

Line R.Title: Install Receptacle Circuits

Summary.Content: Receptacle installation is high-volume, high-visibility work: correctly polarized, solidly terminated, at consistent heights, on circuits that match the plans.

Brass terminal takes the hot, silver takes the neutral, green takes the bond. Loop conductors clockwise under screws, or use the clamp plates — never rely on push-in backstabs for lasting connections. Tamper-resistant receptacles are the residential default.
Trimming out a floor: device heights uniform, faces plumb, terminations torqued snug, then every receptacle verified with a plug-in tester before the plates go on.

Line R.Quiz: Installing receptacles: polarity, termination methods, tamper resistance and verification.

Line R.Title: Install Lighting and Switching Circuits

Summary.Content: Lighting circuits combine boxes, switching, luminaires and often occupancy controls — wired so switching interrupts the hot, never the neutral.
The switched conductor is always the ungrounded one. Modern code wants a neutral available in switch boxes for smart controls. Support luminaires from structure, not from the box, when they're heavy.

Wiring a 3-way stairwell with LED fixtures: power to the first switch, travellers up the wall, switched hot to the fixture, and neutrals joined through — mapped on paper before the first cable is cut.

Line R.Quiz: Lighting circuits: switching the hot, neutrals at switch points, fixture support and 3-way wiring.

Line V.Title: Describe Motor Starter Types

Summary.Content: Motor starters connect motors to power safely: manual starters for small loads, magnetic starters for remote and automatic control, and reduced-voltage or electronic starters where inrush must be tamed.
A magnetic starter is a contactor plus overload protection. Across-the-line starting is simple but hits the system with inrush. Soft starters and VFDs ramp voltage or frequency to ease big motors in.
The compressor that dims the lights on start-up is a candidate for a soft starter — the fix lives in the starter choice, not the motor.

Line V.Quiz: Motor starters: manual vs magnetic, across-the-line starting, soft starters and VFDs.

Line V.Title: Describe Overload Protection

Summary.Content: Motors draw dangerous heat-building current when overloaded or stalled — overload relays sense sustained excess current and disconnect the motor before the windings cook.

Overload relays are sized to the motor's nameplate full-load amps and service factor. They ride through brief inrush but trip on sustained overload. Breakers protect the wiring; overloads protect the motor.
A conveyor jamming daily doesn't need a bigger overload — it needs the jam fixed. The relay tripping is the motor being saved, not the problem itself.

Line V.Quiz: Motor overload protection: nameplate sizing, trip classes and the breaker/overload division of labour.

Line V.Title: Install Magnetic Starters

Summary.Content: Installing a magnetic starter means mounting, powering and protecting it: line and load connections, control wiring to the coil, and overloads dialled to the motor's nameplate.

Match the starter's NEMA/IEC size to the motor. Set overload dials to nameplate FLA. Keep power and control wiring separated and labelled. Verify rotation on first start — three-phase motors don't care which way you expected.
Commissioning a fan starter: overloads set, coil voltage confirmed, bump test for rotation, then the run test with clamp meter readings logged against nameplate.

Line V.Quiz: Installing magnetic starters: sizing, overload settings, control wiring and rotation checks.

Line V.Title: Wire a Start/Stop Control Circuit

Summary.Content: The three-wire start/stop circuit is the foundation of motor control: a momentary start button, a momentary stop, and a seal-in contact that keeps the motor running until told otherwise.

The seal-in (holding) contact parallels the start button. The stop button and every safety interlock wire in series ahead of it. Loss of power drops the coil — three-wire control restarts only by command, which is the safety point.
Wiring the classic circuit on the bench: press start, coil pulls in, seal-in holds, press stop, everything drops — then trace why with the print, until the logic is yours for life.

Line V.Quiz: Three-wire control: start/stop buttons, seal-in contacts, series interlocks and restart behaviour.
